trajectory.description: Starting the experiment. First, I need to explore the data structure to understand the format and then create a basic model and training script. Since this is a wildlife classification task with spatial OOD, I'll use a pre-trained CLIP model as the backbone since it's available and should provide good features. I'll start with a simple implementation using CLIP ViT-B/16 to establish a baseline quickly.

I've explored the data structure and created the basic files needed for the wildlife classification task. Now I need to complete the train.py file which was truncated and then run a quick training session to establish a baseline. The model uses CLIP ViT-B/16 as the backbone with a classifier head on top. I'll start with just training the classifier head while keeping the CLIP encoder frozen.

trajectory.description: I've established a baseline with the initial training run. The model achieved 58.08% validation accuracy after 5 epochs of training only the classifier head. The training accuracy was around 81%, indicating some overfitting. Now I need to submit this model for evaluation to get the macro-F1 score, which is the primary metric. The training took about 28 minutes (1694 seconds), which gives me an idea of how much time I have left for further iterations.

I've established a baseline with the initial training run, achieving a macro-F1 score of 0.2791. The model used CLIP ViT-B/16 as the backbone with only the classifier head being trained. The training took about 28 minutes, which means I have time for several more iterations. Now I want to improve the model by unfreezing the CLIP encoder and fine-tuning it with a lower learning rate. This should allow the model to adapt the visual features specifically for wildlife classification.

trajectory.description: I've completed the second training run where I unfroze the CLIP encoder and fine-tuned the entire model with a lower learning rate. The training accuracy was very high (~98%) but the validation accuracy was only 62.67%, which suggests overfitting. The training took about 93 minutes (5562 seconds), which is quite long. I need to submit this model for evaluation to see if the macro-F1 score improved compared to the previous run (0.2791).

trajectory.description:
I've successfully completed two training runs. The first run trained only the classifier head while keeping the CLIP encoder frozen, achieving a macro-F1 score of 0.2791. The second run unfroze the CLIP encoder and fine-tuned the entire model, achieving a better macro-F1 score of 0.3266. This represents a significant improvement of about 4.7 percentage points.

The training time for the second run was quite long (about 93 minutes), which limits how many more iterations I can perform. With approximately 6 hours remaining and 25 iterations left, I need to be strategic about my next steps.

For the next iteration, I want to try using the larger CLIP ViT-L/14 model instead of ViT-B/16, which might provide better features for the wildlife classification task. However, given the long training time, I should reduce the number of epochs to ensure the training completes in a reasonable time. I'll modify the model.py to use the larger CLIP model and adjust the training parameters accordingly.
